evals.prompt: Write a getting-started tutorial that takes a complete beginner through installing our CLI and running their first command successfully.
evals.expected_output: A learning-oriented Diataxis tutorial: a one-sentence promise, prerequisites, numbered steps each with a visible checkable result, and a conclusion pointing onward.

deterministic_checks.description: Has at least two ordered steps

expectations: Opens with a single concrete promise of what the learner will have done by the end
Each step states a visible, checkable result after the action, not just the instruction itself

evals.prompt: I wrote this as a tutorial but reviewers say it reads like reference docs. Show me what's wrong and a corrected version.
evals.expected_output: Identifies mode-mixing (reference/explanation content inside a tutorial) and rewrites it as a single hands-on happy path.

deterministic_checks.description: Names the specific Diataxis mode it drifted into
States that a tutorial is learning-oriented
Corrected version has ordered numbered steps
Corrected version keeps a Prerequisites section
Corrected version has no TODO placeholder markers

expectations: Produces a corrected tutorial with one happy path and no branching decision points
Moves the 'why it works' content out to a linked explanation rather than inlining it

evals.prompt: Turn these raw setup notes into a proper tutorial for onboarding new contributors.
evals.expected_output: A conformant tutorial derived from the notes, with prerequisites, verifiable numbered steps, and a forward-pointing conclusion.

deterministic_checks.description: Has at least two ordered steps
Emits MIF frontmatter with type: procedural

expectations: Reorders the notes into a strictly linear, single happy-path sequence rather than preserving their original grouping
Each step has an observable, checkable result the learner can confirm

evals.prompt: Should this onboarding content be a tutorial, a how-to, or reference? It's about helping new users feel confident with the product.
evals.expected_output: Recommends a tutorial because the goal is learner confidence, and explains the Diataxis distinction.

deterministic_checks.description: Ties the recommendation to learner confidence or learning, not task completion

expectations: Explains the distinction is about reader stance (beginner vs. competent) rather than the topic itself
Offers to draft the tutorial following the promise-then-steps learning-oriented pattern

evals.prompt: Write a tutorial for our onboarding flow, but it needs to cover both the Mac setup and the Windows setup since our other docs list separate steps per OS.
evals.expected_output: A tutorial that keeps a single happy path (e.g. picks one OS or a shared path) instead of branching per platform mid-lesson, per the 'no options, no alternatives' rule.

deterministic_checks.description: Does not use 'choose whichever fits' branching language

expectations: Picks a single concrete path (e.g. one OS) rather than presenting Mac and Windows as parallel branches
Does not include a 'pick your platform' decision point mid-tutorial
Still delivers a complete set of numbered steps with checkable results for the one path chosen

evals.prompt: Here's a tutorial draft with steps but no way for the learner to tell if they did each one right. Add checkpoints so they know they're on track.
evals.expected_output: A revised tutorial with a self-verification checkpoint after each step ('you should now see...'), without turning it into a troubleshooting guide.

deterministic_checks.description: Adds a self-check checkpoint phrase after a step
Does not introduce a troubleshooting section

expectations: Adds a checkpoint after each individual step, not only at the very end
Does not introduce troubleshooting branches or alternative recovery paths for failures
